Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I had to work around a lot of things. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd try to set aside chunks of time where I could actually work through problems and practice, not just read. I aimed for about 22 hours a week, but it wasn't always consistent. Sometimes I'd study late at night after work or other responsibilities, whenever I could grab the time. I tried to batch similar tasks together when possible, like watching all the videos for a module in one sitting.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was coming up. When deadlines were close, I'd cram in more hours, but during quieter weeks I might slack off a bit. I couldn't really stick to a perfect schedule because life kept throwing curveballs at me. Having limited resources meant I sometimes had to prioritize other things over studying. I did try to at least log in a few times a week to stay somewhat connected to the course.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification from a peer in the discussion forums. The peer support group I was part of really helped keep me accountable, so when someone posted a question or shared notes, I'd feel motivated to log in. Sometimes I'd just check in to see if there was anything new I needed to know about. Honestly, without those peer interactions, I probably would have logged in even less.
Q4: It became more routine after I connected with some classmates through the peer support system. We'd kind of check in with each other, and that made me want to log in to see what they were discussing. After a few weeks, I got into the habit of at least opening the LMS every couple of days. It wasn't super consistent, but it felt more natural once I had people to interact with. The social aspect made it stick more than just the course content alone.
Q5: The video lectures were really helpful because I could pause and rewind when I needed to work through something hands-on. I also found the discussion posts useful because my peers would explain things in different ways that sometimes clicked better for me. The practice quizzes were good too when I could actually get to them. Anything that let me actively do something rather than just passively read was more valuable to me as a kinesthetic learner.
Q6: I'll be honest, I ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the optional resources. With limited time and resources, I had to prioritize what would give me the biggest return. Some of the supplementary PDFs felt too dense and I knew I wouldn't absorb them just by reading. I'd sometimes open them and skim, but I wouldn't really engage deeply. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it often got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to doing well on exams or assignments. If I could practice something and immediately see improvement or understanding, that was valuable. I also valued anything where I could interact with others because that kept me motivated. Honestly, with medium stress levels and limited resources, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was no immediate consequence for skipping them. Required ones had that pressure of a deadline or a grade attached, so I'd make sure to do those even when I wasn't feeling it. Optional stuff I'd only do if I had extra time or if someone in my peer group recommended it. The lack of structure around optional activities made it easy to let them slide when things got busy.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study them later without being connected. I also used textbooks from the library and would write out notes by hand to help things stick. Sometimes I'd work through practice problems on paper for hours without touching the LMS. My best learning happened when I was physically doing things, not just clicking through screens.
Q10: There were many times I'd print out materials or work from downloaded files for days without logging in. I'd sit with my notes and work through problems manually, testing myself over and over. My peer study group would sometimes meet in person and we'd work through concepts together. None of that showed up in the system, but it was some of my most productive studying. The LMS only saw me when I was online, not when I was grinding away offline.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty much my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it if possible, and then review it multiple times offline. My internet access isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed when I could. I'd watch the same lecture video three or four times from my downloads while the LMS only showed one view. It was just more practical for my situation.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when multiple assignments piled up and stress hit medium levels. Sometimes I'd feel my motivation dip and it was hard to push through. Work responsibilities and financial pressures would compete for my attention. During those times, I'd still try to stay connected through my peer group, but my actual engagement with course materials dropped. I'd do the bare minimum to get by until I could regroup.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and other urgent things. Confusion about certain topics would make me avoid those modules temporarily. My LMS usage would get really spotty during high-stress periods, even though I was still trying to keep up somehow. The workload sometimes felt impossible to manage with everything else going on.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open but my mind was elsewhere. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, or have a video playing while thinking about other problems. Sometimes I was just too tired to actually process anything. I'd technically be 'active' but not really learning. Those sessions felt like going through the motions just to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is probably why my assignment completion was lower than my exam scores. Sometimes I'd avoid starting something because I wasn't sure how to approach it and didn't want to waste time doing it wrong. Other times, work or personal stuff just took priority and I'd tell myself I'd catch up later. The anxiety of not understanding something fully would make me put it off, even though I knew that made things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, watching videos and reviewing materials intensively. Without a deadline, I'd drift and focus on other things. My login patterns probably showed clear spikes around due dates. It wasn't the healthiest approach, but with everything I was juggling, deadlines were what kept me from falling completely behind.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me when I got it. Positive feedback on exams motivated me to keep pushing, which probably helped me maintain that distinction grade despite lower assignment scores. When I got critical feedback, I'd try to adjust my approach, though sometimes it felt discouraging. I wished there was more timely feedback on assignments because by the time I got it, I'd already moved on to other things. Good feedback from peers in discussions was also really encouraging.
Q18: The course structure was okay but could have been more flexible for someone in my situation. Having 15 online courses meant I had to juggle a lot, and the rigid deadlines didn't always align with my life. The modules that had clear, step-by-step practical components worked better for me. When the structure was too loose, I'd lose track of what I should be doing. Having the peer support intervention really helped give me some external structure.
Q19: The discussion forums and peer interaction features made engagement easier because they gave me a reason to log in beyond just content. What made it harder was when materials were only available online without download options. Long text-heavy readings were tough for me as a kinesthetic learner. The course could have had more interactive, hands-on activities built into the LMS. Also, some of the navigation was confusing and I'd waste time just finding things.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for understanding but struggling to find it. They might be clicking around, watching videos multiple times, trying to grasp concepts but not feeling confident enough to submit work. Or they could be dealing with personal stuff that makes it hard to complete assignments even though they're trying to stay engaged. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxious browsing.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me in some ways. I didn't have super high LMS activity, but I performed well on exams because I did a lot of studying offline. If you can efficiently learn the material through other means and just use the LMS strategically, you don't need to be logged in constantly. Some of my best learning happened away from the computer, working through problems by hand or discussing with peers in person. The LMS is just one tool, not the whole picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and how long you spend on pages. They're decent at tracking submission times and participation in discussions. But they miss all the offline studying, the quality of engagement versus just time spent, and the real understanding happening in someone's head. They can't capture the conversations with peers outside the platform or the hours spent with downloaded materials. It's like measuring only part of the iceberg.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material and trying to understand it, not just going through motions. It's when I'm curious about a topic and want to figure it out, when I'm actively connecting new information to what I already know. For me, it involves doing something—practicing problems, discussing with others, applying concepts. It's not about how many times I logged in; it's about whether my brain was actually working on the content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to stay motivated, or the hours I spent studying from downloaded materials and library books. It wouldn't capture the personal challenges I was managing alongside coursework, or how I had to be strategic about my time because of limited resources. My resilience in pushing through tough weeks, my genuine interest in doing well despite obstacles—none of that shows up in click data. The numbers tell a partial story at best.
